Statistics.statistics? <br>: Statistics is a discipline of study dealing with the collection, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data.
Statistics.a population of a statistical study? <b: The population is the collection of individuals, items, or data under consideration in a statistical study?
Statistics.a sample of a statistical study? <br>: The portion of the population selected for analysis is called the sample.
Statistics.inferential statistics? <br>: Inferential statistics consists of techniques for reaching conclusions about a population based upon information contained in a sample.
Statistics.a variable? <br>: A variable is a characteristic of interest concerning the individual elements of a population or a sample?
Statistics.a quantative variable? <br>: A quantitative variable is determined when the description of the characteristic of interest results in a numeric value.
Statistics.a discrete variable? <br>: A discrete variable is a quantitative variable whose values are countable.
Statistics.a continuous variable? <br>: A continuous variable is a quantitative variable that can assume any numerical value over an interval or over several intervals.
Statistics.a qualitative variable? <br>: A qualitative variable is determined when the description of the characteristic of interest results in a nonnumeric value.
Statistics.four levels of measurement? <br>: Data can be classified into four levels of measurement or scales of measurement: nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio.
Statistics.four scales of measurement? <br>: Data can be classified into four levels of measurement or scales of measurement: nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio.
Statistics.the nominal scale of measurement? <br>: The nominal scale of measurement applies to data that are usd for category identification. This is characterized by data that consists of names, labels or categories only.
Statistics.Can nominal scale data be ordered? <br>: Nominal scale data cannot be arranged in an ordering scheme. The arithmetic operations of addition, subtraction, multiplication and division are not performed on nominal data.
Statistics.the ordinal scale of measurement? <br>: The ordinal scale applies to data that can be arranged in some order, but differences between data values cannot be determined or are meaningless.
Statistics.Can ordinal scale data be ordered? <br>: Ordinal scale data can be arranged in an ordering scheme.
Statistics.the interval scale of measurement? <br>: The interval scale applies to data that can be arranged in some order and for which differences in data values are meaningful.
Statistics.What does zero mean in an interval scale: The value zero is arbitrarily chosen for interval data and does not imply an absense of the characteristic being measured.
Statistics.Are ratios important in interval data? <: Ratios are not meaningful for interval data.
Statistics.the ratio scale of measurement? <br>: The ratio scale of measurement applies to data that can be ranked and for which all arithmetic operations including division can be performed. Division by zero is, of corurse, excluded.
Statistics.notation for the summation of x? <br>: The symbol x is read as the summation of x.
Statistics.raw data? <br>: Raw data is information obtained by observing values of a variable.
Statistics.qualitative data? <br>: Data obtained by observing values of a qualitative variable are referred to as qualitative data.
Statistics.quantitative data? <br>: Data obtained by observing values of a quantatitave variable are referred to as quantitative data.
Statistics.discrete data? <br>: Quantitative data obtained from a discrete variable are also referred to as discrete data.
Statistics.continuous data? <br>: Quantitative data obtained from a continuous variable are aslso referred to as continuous data.
Statistics.a frequency distribution? <br>: A frequency distribution for qualitative data lists all categories and the number of elements that belong to each of the categories.
Statistics.the relative frequency of a category in: The relative frequency of a category is obtained by dividing the frequency for a category by the sum of all the frequencies.
Statistics.the percentage of a category in a frequ: The percentage of a category is obtained by multiplying the relative frequency for that category by 100.
Statistics.a bar graph? <br>: A bar graph is a graph consisting of bars whose heights are the frequencies of the different categories.
Statistics.a pie chart? <br>: A pie chart is a circle, divided into portions which represent the relative frequencies or percentages belonging to different categories.
Statistics.a histogram? <br>: A histogram is a graph that displays the classes on the horizontal axis and the frequencies of the classes on the vertical axis. The frequency of each class is represented by a vertical bar whose height is equal to the frequency of the class.
Statistics.relationship between a histograph and a: A histogram is similar to a bar graph, but a histogram utilizes classes or intervals and frequencies while a bar graph utilizes categories and frequencies.
Statistics.a symmetric histogram? <br>: A symmetric histogram is one that can be divided into two pieces such that each is the mirror image of the other.
Statistics.a cumulative frequency distribution? <b: A cumulative frequency distribution gives the total number of values that fall below various class boundaries of a frequency distribution.
Statistics.a cumulative relative frequency? <br>: A cumulative relarive frequency is obtained by dividing a cumulative frequency by the total number of observations in the data set.
Statistics.a cumulative percentage? <br>: Cumulative percentages are obtained by multiplying the cumulative relative frequencies by 100.
Statistics.an ogive? <br>: An ogive is a graph in which a point is plotted above each class boundary at a height equal to the cumulative frequency corresponding to that boundary.
Statistics.a stem-and-leaf-display? <br>: In a stem-and-leaf-display each value is divided into a stem and a leaf. The leaves for each stem are shown separately. The stem-and-leaf diagram preserves the information on individual observations.
Statistics.ungrouped data? <br>: A data set consisting of the observations for some variable is referred to as raw data or ungrouped data.
Statistics.grouped data? <br>: Data presented in the form of a frequency distribution are called group data.
Statistics.the mean of a population? <br>: The mean of a population is defined as = x / N, where N is the size of the population.
Statistics.the mean of a sample? <br>: The mean of a sample consisting of n observations is = / n.
Statistics.the median of a set of data? <br>: The median of a set of data is the value that divides the bottom 50% of the data from the top 50% of the data. To find the median of a data set, first arrange the data in increasing order. If the number of observations is odd, the median is the number in the middle of the ordered list. If the number of observations is even, the median is the mean of the two values closest to the middle of the ordered list.
Statistics.the mode of a data set? <br>: The mode is the value in a data set that occurs the most often. If no such value exists, we say that the data set has no mode. If two such values exist, we say the data set is bimodal. If three such values exist, we say the data is trimodal.
Statistics.How do the mean, median, and mode of a n: In a normal distribution (bell shaped distribution), the mean, median, and mode are equal and they are located at the center of the curve.
Statistics.the range of a data set? <br>: The range for a data set is equal to the maximum value in the data minus the minimum value in the data set.
Statistics.the variance of a sample of size n? <br:
The variance of a sample of a size n is represented by s2 and is given by
     s2 = (x - )2 / (n - 1).
Statistics.the variance of a population of size N?:
The variance of a population of size N is represented by 2 and is given by
     2 = (x - )2 / N.
Statistics.the standard deviation? <br>: The square root of the variance is called the standard deviation.
Statistics.Chebyshev's theorem? <br>: Chebyshev's theorem provides a useful interpretation of the standard deviation. Chebyshev's theorem states that the fraction of any data set lying within k standard deviations of the mean is at least 1 - (1/k2), where k is a number greater than 1.
Statistics.empirical rule? <br>: The empirical rule states that for a data set having a normal distribution, approximately 68% of the observations lie within one standard deviation of the mean, approximately 95% of the observations lie within two standard deviations of the mean, and approximately 99.7% of the observations lie within three standard deviations of the mean.
Statistics.the coefficient of variation? <br>:
The coefficient of variation is equal to the standard deviation divided by the mean.
     CV = s / or CV = s / .
     The coefficient of variation is a measure of relative variation, whereas the standard deviation is a measure of absolute variation.
Statistics.a z-score? <br>: A z-score is the number of standard deviations that a given observation, x, is below or above the mean.
Statistics.an experiment? <br>: An experiment is any observation or procedure whose outcomes cannot be predicted with certainty.
Statistics.a sample space? <br>: The set of all possible outcomes for an experiment is called the sample space.
Statistics.counting rule? <br>: The counting rule for a two-step experiment states that if the first step can result in any one of n1 outcomes, and the second step in any one of n2 outcomes, then the experiment can result in (n1)(n2) outcomes.
Statistics.an event?<br>: An event is a subset of the sample space consisting of at least one outcome from the sample space.
Statistics.a simple event? <br>: A simple event is an event that consists of exactly one outcome.
Statistics.a compound event? <br>: If an event consists of more than one outcome, it is called a compound event.
Statistics.probability? <br>: Probability is the measure of the likelihood of the occurrence of some event.
Statistics.nontation for the probability of an even: The probability of an event E is represented by the symbol P(E).
Statistics.can the probability of an event take? <b: The probability of an event is a real number between zero and one, i.e. 0 P(E) 1
Statistics.about the sum of the probabilities of th:
The sum of the probabilities for all the simple events of an experiment must equal one. i.e.
     If E1, ..., En are the simple events for an experiment, then the following must be true: P(E1) + ... + P(En) = 1
Statistics.a joint probability? <br>: Joint probability is the probability of two events occurring in conjunction. That is, it is the probability of both events together. The joint probability of A and B is written P(A B), P(AB), or P(A, B).
Statistics.a marginal probability? <br>: Given two events, A and B, the marginal probability of A is the probability of the event A P(A), that is, the probability of A, regardless of whether event B did or did not occur. This can be represented as P(A) = P(A B) + P(A B'),
Statistics.a conditional probability? <br>:
A conditional probability is the probability of an event, assuming a particular set of circumstances. In particular, it is the probability of the occurrence of event A given that event B is known to have occurred for some experiment. This is denoted P(A | B).and the following formula is used to compute conditional probability:
     P(A | B) = P(A B) / P(B)
Statistics.mutually exclusive events? <br>: Two or more events are said to be mutually exclusive if the events do not have any outcomes in common, i.e. the events cannot occur together.
Statistics.relationship between mutually exclusive: If A and B are mutually exclusive events, then P(A B) = 0.
Statistics.dependent events? <br>: If the knowledge that some event B has occurred influences the probability of the occurrence of the event A, then A and B are said to be dependent events.
Statistics.independent events? <br>: If the knowledge that some event B has occurred does not influence the probability of the occurrence of the event A, then A and B are said to be independent events.
Statistics.relationship between independent events:
Two events are independent if the following equation is satisfied:
     P(A | B) = P(A).
Statistics.the compliment of an event? <br>: To every event, there corresponds another event, Ac, called the compliment of A. This event consists of all outcomes that are in the sample space that are not in event A. A' is also used to indicate Ac.
Statistics.the intersection of two events? <br>: The intersection of two events A and B consists of all those outcomes which are common to both A and B, and is represented by the notation A B.
Statistics.multiplication rule? <br>: The probability of the intersection of two events is given by the multiplication rule and is defined as P(A B) = P(A)*P(B | A).
Statistics.relationship between independent events: If A and B are independent events, then P(A B) = P(A)P(B)
Statistics.the union of two events? <br>: The union of two events A and B consists of all those outcomes that belong to A or B or both A and B. This is represented A B.
Statistics.probability of the union of two events?:
The probability of the union of two events is denoted P(A B) and is defined by the formula
     P(A B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A B). This is called the addition rule.
Statistics.addition rule? <br>:
The probability of the union of two events is denoted P(A B) and is defined by the formula
     P(A B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A B). This is called the addition rule.
Statistics.probability of the union of two mutually: If A and B are mutually exclusive events, then P(A B) = P(A) + P(B).
Statistics.Bayes' Theorem? <br>:
Bayes' Theorem (or Bayes' Rule) gives an alternative equation for the conditional probability of two events:
     P(A | B) = (P(B | A) P(A)) / P(B)
Statistics.a collectively exhaustive set of events: A set of events are collectively exhaustive if they are mutually exclusive and their union equals the sample space.
Statistics.a random variable? <br>: A random variable associates a numerical value with each outcome of an experiment. This is a real-valued function whose domain is a sample space.
Statistics.a discrete random variable? <br>: A random variable is a discrete random variable if it has a countable number of values.
Statistics.a continuous random variable? <br>: A random variable is a continuous random variable if it is capable of assuming all the values in an interval or several intervals.
Statistics.a probability distribution function? <b: The probability distribution of a discrete random variable is a list of the values in the sample space and the probabilities associated with those values.
Statistics.the mean (expected value) of a discrete: The mean of a discrete random variable is given by = x X P(x), where X is our sample space and is denoted E(X).
Statistics.the variance of a discrete random varia: The varince of a discrete random variable is given by 2 = x X (x - )2 P(x). It is also represented by Var(X).
Statistics.the standard deviation of a discrete ra: The standard deviation of a discrete random variable is represented by and is given by = (Var(X)).
Statistics.a binomial random variable? <br>: A binomial random variable is a discrete random variable that is defined when the conditions of a binomial experiment are satisfied.
Statistics.conditions of a binomial experiment? <br:
The conditions of a binomial experiment are:
     1. There are n identical trials.
     2. Each trial has only two possible outcomes.
     3. The probabilities of the two outcomes remain constant for each trial.
     4. The trials are independent.
     
     The two outcomes on each trial are called success and failure and have associated probabilities p and q (p + q = 1).
Statistics.the binomial probability formula? <br>:
The binomial probability formula is used to compute probabilities for binomial random vairables. The binomial distribution is
     P(x) = nCr(n, x)px(1-p)n-x for x = 0, 1, 2, …, n.
Statistics.mean of a binomial random variable? <br>: The mean of a binomial random variable is given by = np, where n is the number of trials and p is the probability of success.
Statistics.probability of the variance of of a bino: The variance of a binomial random variable is given by 2 = npq, where n is the number of trials, p is the probability of success, and q = 1 - p.
Statistics.When is the binomial distribution applic: The binomial random variable is applicable when counting the number of occurrences of an event in a finite or small number of trials.
Statistics.When is the poisson distribution applica: The poisson random variable is applicable when counting the number of occurrences of an event in a large or infinite number of trials.
Statistics.a Poisson distribution? <br>: The possion random variable is given by P(x) = (xe-) / x!, for x = 0, 1, 2, ... , and is the number of trials times the probability of success in a trial.
Statistics.mean of a Poisson random variable? <br>: The mean of a Poisson random variable is given by = .
Statistics.variance of a Poisson random variable? <: The variance of a Poisson random variable is given by 2 = .
Statistics.When is the hypergeometric distribution: The hypergeometric distribution is used when there is success or failure on each trial, but where there is not independence from trial to trial.
Statistics.a hypergeometric random variable? <br>: When n items are selected from N items of which k are successes and N - k are failures, the random variable X, defined to equal the number of successes in the n selected items, is a hypergeometric random variable.
Statistics.hypergeometric distribution? <br>:
The probibility distribution of a hypergeometric random variable is given by
     P(X) = (nCr(k, x)*nCr(N-k, n-x)) / nCr(N, n), for x = 0, 1, ...,n.
Statistics.a probability density function?<br>: The probability density function of a continuous random variable is a function that describes the relative likelihood for this random variable to occur at a given point.
Statistics.the continuous uniform distribution? <b: The continuous uniform distribution (also called the rectangular distribution) is a family of probability density functions such that for any member of the family, all intervals of the same length with nonzero probability are equally probable.
Statistics.probability density function of the cont: The probability density function of the continuous uniform distribution is f(x) = 1 / (b-a) for a x b, and f(x) = 0 for x a or x b, where a and b are the minimum and maximum values on which the distribution is defined.
Statistics.mean of a continuous uniform distributio: The mean of a continuous uniform distribution is given by = (a + b) / 2, where it is defined over the interval from a to b.
Statistics.variance of a continuous uniform distrib: The variance for a continuous uniform distribution is given by 2 = (b - a)2 / 12.
Statistics.the normal distribution? <br>: A random variable X has a normal distribution and it is referred to as a normal random variable if and only if its probability density is given by P(X) = 1 / ((2)) e-(1/2)((x - )/)2 for - < x < , where > 0.
Statistics.the standard normal distribution? <br>: The normal distribution with = 0 and = 1 is referred to as the standard normal distribution.
Statistics.symmetry of the normal distribution? <br: The curve of the normal distribution is symmetric about the mean.
Statistics.tails of the normal distribution? <br>: The tails of the curve of the normal distribution extend indefinitely.
Statistics.highest point on a normal distribution's: The highest point on a normal distribution curve occurs at the mean.
Statistics.an exponential distribution? <br>: A random variable X has an exponential distribution and it is referred to as an exponential random variable if and only if its probability density is given by P(X) = (1/)e-x/ for x > 0, and P(x) = 0 otherwise.
Statistics.a random sample? <br>: If X1, ..., Xn are independent and identically distributed random variables, we say that they constitute a random sample from the infinite population given by their common distribution.
Statistics.a statistic? <br>: A statistic is a random variable that is a function of a set of random variables X1, ..., Xn, constituting a random sample.
Statistics.a sampling distribution?<br>: The probability distribution of a statistic is called a sampling distribution.
Statistics.the sample mean? <br>: If X1, ..., Xn constitute a random sample, then = (1/n)I = 1 to nXi is called the sample mean.
Statistics.the sample variance? <br>: If X1, ..., Xn constitute a random sample, then S2 = (1/(n-1))I = 1 to n(Xi - ) is called the sample variance.
Statistics.mean of a sampling distribution of the m: If X1, ..., Xn constitute a random sample form an infinite population with the mean , then E() =
Statistics.variance of a sampling distribution of t: If X1, ..., Xn constitute a random sample form an infinite population with the variance 2, then var() = 2/n
Statistics.the standard error of the mean? <br>: The standard error of the mean is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the mean, given by X = /(n), where is the standard deviation of the underlying distribution.
Statistics.law of large numbers? <br>: The law of large numbers states that for any positive constant c, the probability that will take on a value between - c and + c is at least 1 - 2/nc2. When n approaches , this probability approaches 1.
Statistics.central limit theorem?<br>: The central limit theorem states that if X1, ..., Xn constitute a random sample from an infinite population with mean and variance 2, then the limiting distribution of Z = ( - ) / ( /(n)) as n approaches is the standard normal distribution.
